svg.text: Floyd's Cycle Math — Why Reset-and-Walk Finds the Entry
Let µ = distance from head to cycle entry, λ = cycle length.
When slow has taken k steps and fast 2k steps, both inside the cycle:
slow position = (k - µ) mod λ (offset from cycle entry)
At meeting: (2k - µ) ≡ (k - µ) mod λ ⟹ k ≡ 0 mod λ ⟹ k is a multiple of λ.
So slow is k - µ steps into the cycle, where k is a multiple of λ.
Equivalently: slow is at position −µ mod λ from the cycle entry.
A walker starting at head needs µ steps to reach the entry. A walker at meeting also needs
µ steps (mod λ) to reach the entry. So one-step walks from both meet at the entry.
Phase 1: slow = head, fast = head. Loop: slow = slow.next, fast = fast.next.next.
If fast or fast.next is null → no cycle. If slow == fast → cycle found.
Phase 2: slow = head (keep fast at meeting). Loop: advance both by one. Return slow.
Cycle length λ: from meeting, advance one pointer until it returns; count steps.
Brent's algorithm is an alternative: typically faster constant factor, same O(n) bound.
